docs.title: MintMousse
docs.text:
MintMousse is a web console for [LÖVE][love] that lets you monitor and interact with your project through a browser.

It runs a local web server alongside your game, providing a dedicated space to inspect values, tweak game states, and visualise data in real-time. It's built to replace the cluttered print statements and make-shift in-game UIs, while acting as a remote interface for headless servers.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Visual Debugging - Move past print statements with a fully featured colored logging system
  • Live Interaction - Trigger events or toggle flags with buttons and sliders in the browser while the game is running
  • Thread Support - Update your dashboard and log from any thread without worrying about conflicts
  • Headless Support - Manage and monitor dedicated servers or windowless projects seamlessly across the network with full IPv6/dual-stack support
  • Extendable Components - Add your own custom components, allowing you to make the dashboard work for you

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text:
First, add the library to your project: 

cd your/love/project/directory
git clone https://github.com/EngineerSmith/MintMousse libs/MintMousse
Next, add the preload script to conf.lua: 
require("libs.MintMousse.preload")
Then, add the "Hello World" example to main.lua: 
local mintmousse = require("libs.MintMousse")
mintmousse.start({ whitelist = "localhost" })

local dashboard = mintmousse.newTab("Dashboard")
dashboard:newCard({ text = "Hello World!" })
Finally, run your project and view the console in your browser at http://localhost:8080 (or the port shown in your console)

docs.title: Config
docs.text:
As MintMousse is a threaded library, a lot of what can be configured, can only be done before run-time. Therefore it's recommended you edit the file conf.lua directly - you can always grab the default from git again if you break something badly. As MintMousse is a threaded library, almost all configuration must be done before the library is first required. The easiest way is to edit the values directly in the library's conf.lua (you can always restore the default version from the GitHub repo if needed.)

Validation

None of the values are type-checked or validated at runtime. Incorrect values will cause crashes or unexpected behaviour.

docs.title: mintmousse.COMPONENT_PATHS
docs.text:
Type: table(array of strings)Default: { DIRECTORY_PATH .. "components/" }

Directories to search for components. Later entries override earlier ones on a per-file basis. Use this to add your own components or to include a git repo that provides extra components. See Components to see how to create your own. -- TODO link

docs.title: mintmousse.DEFAULT_INDEX_HTML
docs.text:
Type: stringDefault: DIRECTORY_PATH .. "thread/index.html"

Path to the base HTML file severed to connecting clients. This is a Mustache template.

docs.title: mintmousse.DEFAULT_INDEX_JS
docs.text:
Type: stringDefault: DIRECTORY_PATH .. "thread/index.js"

Path to the base JavaScript file served to clients. This is a Mustache template.

docs.title: mintmousse.DEFAULT_INDEX_CSS
docs.text:
Type: stringDefault: DIRECTORY_PATH .. "thread/index.css"

Path to base CSS file served to clients. This is a Mustache template.

docs.title: RFG Icons
docs.text: These two settings are only relevant if you use RFG icons.

docs.title: `mintmousse.ZIP_MOUNT_LOCATION
docs.text:
Type: stringDefault: `".MintMousse/"

Directory used by love.filesystem.mount() when mounting ZIP archieves that contain RFG icons.

docs.title: mintmousse.FAVICON_PATH
docs.text:
Type: stringDefault: "/favicon"

HTTP endpoint where the favicon assets are served. This must match the "favicon path" you set when generating icons on the RFG website.

docs.title: mintmousse.SOCKET_BACKLOG
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 32

Maximum number of pending connections the OS will queue before refusing new ones.

docs.title: mintmousse.MAX_HTTP_RECEIVE_SIZE
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 2^16 (65KB)

The maximum allowed size of an incoming HTTP request body. Larger requests are rejected and the limit is included in the response headers.

docs.title: mintmousse.MAX_WEBSOCKET_FRAME_SIZE
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 2^19 (512KB)

Maximum size of a single WebSocket Frame (both incoming and outgoing).

docs.title: mintmousse.MAX_WEBSOCKET_MESSAGE_SIZE
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 2^21 (2MB)

Maximum size of a complete WebSocket message (after reassembling fragmented frames).

docs.title: mintmousseCACHE_CONTROL_HEADER
docs.text:
Type: stringDefault: "no-store"

Value of the Cache-Control header sent for static assets. Change to something like "public, max-age=3600" for production.

docs.title: mintmousse.MAX_PORT_ATTEMPTS
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 100

Maximum number of sequential port increments tried when autoIncrement = true is passed to mintmousse.start.

docs.title: mintmousse.TIMEOUT_HTTP
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 30

Idle timeout for HTTP connections. A 408 Request Timeout is sent if exceeded.

docs.title: mintmousse.TIMEOUT_WEBSOCKET
docs.text:
Type: number (seconds)Default: 60

Idle timeout for WebSocket connections (includes the internal heartbeat mechanism).

docs.title: mintmousse.PING_WEBSOCKET
docs.text:
Type: number (seconds)Default: 30

Interval between WebSocket ping frames (used for keep-alive).

docs.title: mintmousse.COMPONENT_PARSE_TIMEOUT
docs.text:
Type: number (seconds)Default: 3

Maximum time to wait for component type parsing. If you see a timeout warning, increase this value or use the preload script.

docs.title: mintmousse.REPLACE_DEFAULT_ERROR_HANDLER
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: true

If true, MintMousse replaces Love’s default error handler with its own (adds better logging and cleaned-up stack traces).

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_ENABLED_TIMESTAMP
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: true

Prefix every log with a timestamp.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_TIMESTAMP_FORMAT
docs.text:
Type: stringDefault: "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f"

Format passed to os.date. MintMousse adds the custom %f specifier (milliseconds, 3 digits padded). Performance is best when %f appears at the very end of the string.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_ENABLE_STREAM_OUT
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: true

Enable logging to io.stdout / io.stderr (behaves like the global print). Automatically strips ANSI colours when the stream is redirected to a file.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_ENABLE_ERROR
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: true

When logger:error() is called, also call the global error() function.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_INCLUDE_TRACE
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: false

Include debug info (function@file:line) in all log message levels. Has a noticeable performance cost with each lookup.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_WARNINGS_CAUSE_ERRORS
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: false

If true, warning logs are promoted to error logs.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_WARNINGS_INCLUDE_TRACE
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: false

Include trace information on warning logs (does not effect other log levels).

docs.title: mintmousse.REPLACE_FUNC_PRINT
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: true

Replaces the global function print with logger:debug. The original print is still available as GLOBAL_print (but it isn't considered thread-safe).

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_CLEAR_UP_TRACEBACK
docs.text:
Type: booleanDefault: true

Clean internal MintMousse and Love calls from the traceback shown by MintMousse.logUncaughtError. It doesn't effect the public MintMousse.cleanupTraceback function.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_BUFFER_SIZE
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 2^20 (1MB)

Size of the stdout buffer. Increase if you see garbled/interleaved output, or call mintmousse.flushLogs more often.

docs.title: mintmousse.LOG_MAX_PENDING_LOGS_PER_FLUSH
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 512

Maximum number of logs the global sinks will process in a single call to mintmousse.flushLogs (only runs on the main thread).

docs.title: MintMousse Thread Settings
docs.text: You probably won't need to change these unless you have very specific performance requirements.

docs.title: mintmousse.MAX_THREAD_MESSAGES
docs.text:
Type: numberDefault: 100

Maximum commands the MintMousse thread will process in one loop iteration.

docs.title: mintmousse.THREAD_SLEEP
docs.text:
Type: number (seconds)Default: 1e-4 (0.0001s)

sleep time between thread loop iterations. Increase to reduce CPU usage, decrease if the thread feels sluggish.

docs.title: Channel / Event IDs
docs.text: Only change these if they conflict with IDs already used elsewhere in your project.

docs.title: Channels
docs.text: Channel ID Description READONLY_THREAD_LOCATION Holds the MintMousse thread Object (read-only) THREAD_COMMAND_QUEUE_ID Queue for commands sent to the MintMousse thread READONLY_BUFFER_DICTIONARY_ID Internal string.buffer dictionary THREAD_ID_COUNTER Counter for assigning unique thread IDs READONLY_BASIC_TYPES_ID Parsed component types used by proxy tables LOCK_LOG_BUFFER_FLUSH Mutex lock for stdout buffer LOCK_LOG_BUFFER_ERR Mutex lock for stderr LOG_EVENT_CHANNEL Used to queue global sink logs

docs.title: Events
docs.text: Event ID Description errBufferLockChannel love.event used by the MintMousse thread to communicate back to the main thread

docs.title: Components
docs.text: Documentation for individual component types and fields

docs.title: Types
docs.text: Type Description Component Component Instance, also known as a Proxy Table

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Description mintmousse.newTab Create a new Tab mintmousse.get Get a component using given ID mintmousse.has Check if a component exists on the current thread using given ID mintmousse.remove Remove a component using given ID mintmousse.onEvent Add a callback for event mintmousse.removeEvent Remove a callback for an event

docs.title: mintmousse.get
docs.text: Use this function to get a component with a given ID. This function will always return a component with the given ID. You can use the typeHint parameter to suggest what type this component is if it doesn't exist on the local thread.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: component = mintmousse.get( id, typeHint )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: id string The ID of the component you're trying to get a reference of. typeHint string If the component is not found locally, it create's a local version with the given hint. This hint is ignored if it found.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: component Component The returned component with the given ID. It will always return a component that can be updated.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local list = mintmousse.get("playerList")
list.isNumbered = true

local dbList = mintmousse.get("dashboardList", "List")
list:newContainer()

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • mintmousse.has

docs.title: mintmousse.has
docs.text: Use this function to check if a component exists locally already. Useful if you want to use mintmousse.get, but not accidentally create a new component.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: doesExist = mintmousse.has( id )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: id string The ID of the component you're trying to check if it exists locally.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: doesExist boolean If the given ID already has a component table on the current thread.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
if mintmousse.has("dashboardTab") then
  local dashboard = mintmousse.get("dashboardTab")
end

if not mintmousse.has("foobar") then
  mintmousse.get("foobar", "Accordion")
end

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • mintmousse.get

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: tab = mintmousse.newTab( title, id, index )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: title string ("UNKNOWN") The name of the tab you want to create id string (nil) The ID to use for the new tab. If one isn't given, one is generated. index number (-1) The index you want to insert the new tab into the navbar. By default, it is placed at the end of the navbar.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: tab Component The newly created tab.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local tab = mintmousse.newTab("Dashboard", "dbTab", 1)

local console = mintmousse.newTab("Console", nil, -1)

local unknown = mintmousse.newTab(nil, nil, -2)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Components

docs.title: mintmousse.onEvent
docs.text:
Used to assign callback functions to callback IDs.

Thread behaviour

This function can only be called on the main thread. Thus, all events are piped back to the main thread with a snapshot of the component if it doesn't exist on the main thread allowing you to make general changes, or get values - but it won't update the thread the component was originally created on with any changes you make to this temp object.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.onEvent( callbackID, callbackFunction )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: callbackID string The ID used to direct events callbackFunction function The function called when an event occurs for a specific callbackID

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.onEvent("myButtonEvent", function(component)
  component.text = component.text .. "!"
  if component.type == "Button" then
    component.color = component.color == "primary" and "secondary" or "primary"
  end
end)

mintmousse.onEvent("myImportEvent", function(component)
  print(component.id .. " triggered an important event!")
end)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • mintmousse.removeEvent

docs.title: mintmousse.remove
docs.text:
Remove a component using it's id. If the component doesn't exist, it is ignored. See (Component):remove if you want to remove a component you already have a table for.

isDead

Once this method, or (Component):remove is called, and there is an existing component table with the given ID. It's field isDead becomes true, and you will no longer be able to set, or get any field. The same is true for all it's known-locally children.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.remove( id )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: id string A valid ID for removal.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.remove("dashboardList")

if not mintmousse.has("dashboardList") then
  print("Removed dashboardList")
end

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • (Component):remove

docs.title: mintmousse.removeEvent
docs.text:
Used ot removed any functions assigned to a given callback ID.

Thread behaviour

This function can only be called on the main thread. As no callback details are kept on individual threads.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.removeEvent( callbackID )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: callbackID string The ID to clear any assigned function from.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: mintmousse.removeEvent("myButtonEvent")

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • mintmousse.onEvent

docs.title: Component
docs.text: The object you use to update the components on the web console. It is also known as a Proxy Table.

docs.title: Constructors
docs.text: Constructors Description mintmousse.newTab Create a new Tab (Component):new<Type> Create a new component

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Description (Component):add<Type> Create a new component (Component):remove Removes itself, and it's children if any (Component):setChildrenOrder Set the order of all the children at once (Component):moveBefore Move the current component before the given component (Component):moveAfter Move the current component after the given component (Component):moveToFront Move the component to the 1st index (Component):moveToBack Move the component to the last index (Component):children Used to iterate over all the locally known children

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Description (Component).id The ID of the component, one is generated if one isn't given (Component).type The type of the component, or "unknown" if it isn't known (Component).parentID The parent ID of the component, if the local thread knows it (Component).parent Get the parent component of this component (Component).back Syntax sugar: alias for (Component).parent (Component).isDead Has this component marked removed.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Components

docs.title: (Component):add<Type>
docs.text: Used to create a new component, but returns the parent component so it can be chained.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: parentComponent = parentComponent:add<Type>(component)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Type string This is the component type that you want to create. component Component The initial values for the component, such as id.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: parentComponent Component Returns the caller table, so it can be chained

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local list = tab:newList()
  :addText({ text = "My awesome List" })
  :addAlert({ text = "We can add siblings easily!" })
  :newContainer()
    :newCardBody()
      :addCardTitle({ text = "Card title" })
      :addCardText({ text = "Card Text" })
      :addCardFooter({ text = "Card Text" })
      .back
    .back
  :addText({ text = "The bottom of the list" })

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component
  • (Component):new<Type>

docs.title: (Component):children
docs.text: Used to iterate over the locally known children.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: itFunc, childList, index = component:children()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: This function returns an iterator that can be used directly like ipairs. You shouldn't alter the order of the children directly, and use one of the many move helper functions. However, don't call move functions within the loop, as you can break the ordering.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
for i, child in component:children() do
  print(i, child.id)
end

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component

docs.title: (Component):moveAfter
docs.text: Move the component after one of it's siblings.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: component = component:moveAfter( sibling )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: sibling string or Component Can be an ID of a sibling, or the sibling component itself.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: component Component Returns the caller table, so it can be chained

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local container = mintmousse.get("container")
container.moveAfter("otherContainer")

container.moveAfter(mintmousse.get("container"))

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component
  • (Component):moveBefore

docs.title: (Component):moveBefore
docs.text: Move the component before one of it's siblings.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: component = component:moveBefore( sibling )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: sibling string or Component Can be an ID of a sibling, or the sibling component itself.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: component Component Returns the caller table, so it can be chained

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local playerComponent = mintmousse.get("player.john")
playerComponent:moveBefore("player.steven")

playerComponent:moveBefore(otherPlayerComponent)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component
  • (Component):moveAfter

docs.title: (Component):moveToBack
docs.text: Move the component to back/bottom/last most position.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: component = component:moveToBack()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: component Component Returns the caller table, so it can be chained.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local playerContainer = mintmouse.get("player.steven", "container")
playerContainer:moveToBack()

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component
  • (Component):moveToFront

docs.title: (Component):moveToFront
docs.text: Move the component to front/top/first most position.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: component = component:moveToFront()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: component Component Returns the caller table, so it can be chained.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local playerList = mintmousse.get("playerList")
playerList:moveToFront()

local tab = mintmousse.get("tab", "Tab")
tab:moveToFront()

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component
  • `(Component):moveToBack

docs.title: (Component):new<Type>
docs.text: Used to create a new Component, and return that new component.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: newComponent = parentComponent:new<Type>(component)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Type string This is the component type that you want to create. component Component The initial values for the component, such as id.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: newComponent Component The newly created component.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local card = tab:newCard()

local list = card:newList({ id = "myList", isNumbered = true })

list:newContainer()
  :newText({ text = "Hello world" })
    .back
  :newText({ text = "Example" })

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component
  • (Component):add<Type>

docs.title: (Component).parent
docs.text:
A field of Component that lets you grab the parent component table, if the component knows it's parent.

Type

This field can be nil in such cases where the local thread doesn't know the parent of this component, or it is a root component of type Tab.

docs.title: Alias
docs.text: (Component).back This alias is to make chaining functions more readable. But you can still use .parent if it's your preference.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
tab:newCard({ size = 5, title = "Info" })
  :newContainer()
    :addText({ text = "Hello World" })
    :addText({ text = "I like Mousse!" })
    .back
  :newCardBody() -- Call on the `Card` component
    :addCardTitle({ text = "Card Title" })
    :addCardText({ text = "Card Text" })
    :addCardFooter({ text = "Card footer" })
    .parent
  :newContainer()
    :addText({ text = "I really like Mint flavoured Mousse!" })

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component

docs.title: (Component):remove
docs.text:
Used to remove a component.

isDead

Once this method, or mintmousse.remove is called. The component table's field isDead becomes true, and you will no longer be able to set, or get any other field. The same is true for all it's known-locally children.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: component:remove()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local tab = mintmousse.newTab("Remove me")

tab:remove()
if not tab.isDead then
  tab:addText({ text = "This will never run" })
end

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component
  • mintmousse.remove

docs.title: (Component):setChildrenOrder
docs.text: Used to reorder all children that a component has, even if they're not declared locally either via creation or using mintmousse.get.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: component = component:setChildrenOrder( newOrder )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: newOrder table of string The array of IDs in the order you want them to appear.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local list = tab:newList({ isNumbered = true })
  :addText({ id = "0", text = "sweetie" })
  :addText({ id = "1", text = "this" })
  :addText({ id = "2", text = "make" })
  :addText({ id = "3", text = "Does" })
  :addText({ id = "4", text = "sense" })
  :addText({ id = "5", text = "?" })

list:setChildrenOrder({
  "3", "1", "2", "4"
}) -- If IDs are missing, they are moved to the end in their relative order before the sort

for _, c in list:children() do
  print(c.text) --> Does this make sense sweetie ?
end

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Components
  • Component

docs.title: Component Types
docs.text:
All built-in MintMousse components.

Type Updates Pushes Events Children Description

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Component

docs.title: Button
docs.text: TODO

docs.title: Updates
docs.text: color BSColor ("primary") A bootstrap color for the entire button. colorOutline boolean (false) If the button should be outlined instead of solid fill. text string ("") Text the button displays isDisabled boolean (false) If the button should be disabled (faded out, unable to be pressed). width BSWidth ("100") The percentage width the button takes up. Text wraps downward. isCentered _boolean` (true) Centers the button

docs.title: Events
docs.text: onEventClick callbackID The callback ID to trigger. See mintmousse.onEvent

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
{
  text = "Press me!",
  color = "secondary",
  onEventClick = "myButtonCallback",
}

{
  text = "My fancy button!",
  color = "warning",
  colorOutline = true,
  width = "50",
  isCentered = true,
  onEventClick = "myButtonCallback,
}

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Button Group

docs.title: Controller
docs.text: Using these functions you can start, and stop the library.

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Description mintmousse.start Start the web server mintmousse.stop Stop the MM thread mintmousse.wait Wait for the MM thread to stop mintmousse.setIcon Set the web console's icon mintmousse.setIconRaw Set the web console's icon directly mintmousse.setIconRFG Set the web console's icon to an RFG zip file mintmousse.setTitle Set the web console's title mintmousse.notify Send a toast notification to connected clients mintmousse.addToWhitelist Add a rule to the whitelist mintmousse.removeFromWhitelist Remove a rule from the whitelist mintmousse.clearWhitelist Clear all rules from the whitelist mintmousse.batchStart Start batching changes mintmousse.batchEnd End batching changes, and send to thread mintmousse.buildPage Add a preconfigured tab mintmousse.getThreadID Get the current thread's ID

docs.title: Variables
docs.text: Variable Description love.isThread Boolean if code is running in a thread MINTMOUSSE_DO_NOT_START Global boolean if thread shouldn't start automatically when Preload is required THREAD_ID Global string for the ID to use for the current thread

docs.title: mintmousse.addToWhitelist
docs.text:
Used to add new rules to the whitelist. The whitelist is always enforced and cannot be turned off. Use this to grant specific IP addresses or network ranges access to the web console.

Rule Type Example Logic Loopback Aliases "localhost" Automatically whitelist both 127.0.0.1 and ::1 IPv4 Host "198.168.1.5" Matches a single specific IPv4 address IPv6 Host "2001:db8::cafe" Matches a single specific IPv6 address (supports compression) IPv4 Range "10.0.0.0/8" Standard CIDR notation for IPv4 subnets IPv6 Range "2001:db8::/32" Standard CIDR notation for IPv6 subnets IPv4-Mapped "::ffff:172.16.0.1" Supports IPv4 addresses wrapped in IPv6 for tunneled traffic

!!! info "Pro-active Protection With no whitelist entries, no connections will be accepted. It is highly recommended to at least include "localhost" during development.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.addToWhitelist( additions )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: additions string or table (nil) The rule(s) you want to add. * If a string is passed, it is treated as a single rule. * If a table is passed, it must be a continuous array of strings.

docs.title: Rule Definitions
docs.text: A rule can be one of the following: - Aliases: "localhost", "local", "127.0.0.1", or "::1". All four strings perform the same action: whitelisting the IPv4 and IPv6 loopback addresses. - IPv4 Address: A standard dot-decimal address (e.g. "192.168.4.27") - IPv6 Address: A standard colon-separated hex address. Supports compression (e.g. "2001:db8::cafe") - IPv4 CIDR: An IPv4 range (e.g. "192.168.0.0/16") - IPv6 CIDR: An IPv6 range (e.g. "2001:db8::/32") - IPv4-mapped IPv6: An IPv4 address expressed in IPv6 format (e.g. "::ffff:172.16.0.1")

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.addToWhitelist("localhost")

mintmousse.addToWhitelist({
  "local",
  "10.0.0.0/8",
})

mintmousse.addToWhitelist({
  "localhost",         -- Covers loopback for v4 and v6
  "192.168.4.27",      -- Specific IPv4 address
  "192.168.0.0/16",    -- IPv4 subnet
  "2001:db8::/32",     -- IPv6 subnet
  "2001:db8::cafe",    -- Single IPv6 Host
  "::ffff:127.16.0.1", -- IPv4-mapped IPv6 support
})

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.start
  • `mintmousse.removeFromWhitelist

docs.title: mintmousse.batchEnd
docs.text:
Used to end batching changes into a single thread message. This function also processes all the current batched messages, and could take a moment to process on large amount of changes (but overall takes less time than individual changes).

See [mintmousse.batchStart] to start a batch.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.batchEnd()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.batchStart()

local tab = mintmousse.newTab("Dashboard")
local list = tab:newCard({ title = "Players" }):newList()
for _, player in ipairs(server.connectedPlayers) do
  list:newContainer({ id = "dashboardList_" .. player.username })
    :addCardTitle(player.username)
    :newRow()
      :newContainer({ columnWidth = 4 }):newRow()
        :addText({ text = "Health" })
        :addText({ text = player.health })
      .back
      :newContainer({ columnWidth = 4 }):newRow()
        :addText({ text = "Mana" })
        :addText({ text = player.mana })
      .back.back
      :newContainer({ columnWidth = 4 }):newRow()
        :addText({ text = "Kills" })
        :addText({ text = player.kills })
end

mintmousse.batchEnd()

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • `mintmousse.batchEnd

docs.title: mintmousse.batchStart
docs.text:
Used to batch huge amount of changes into a single thread message. Useful for when first constructing an entire tab.

See [mintmousse.batchEnd] to end a batch.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.batchStart()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.batchStart()

local tab = mintmousse.newTab("Dashboard")
local list = tab:newCard({ title = "Players" }):newList()
for _, player in ipairs(server.connectedPlayers) do
  list:newContainer({ id = "dashboardList_" .. player.username })
    :addCardTitle(player.username)
    :newRow()
      :newContainer({ columnWidth = 4 }):newRow()
        :addText({ text = "Health" })
        :addText({ text = player.health })
      .back
      :newContainer({ columnWidth = 4 }):newRow()
        :addText({ text = "Mana" })
        :addText({ text = player.mana })
      .back.back
      :newContainer({ columnWidth = 4 }):newRow()
        :addText({ text = "Kills" })
        :addText({ text = player.kills })
end

mintmousse.batchEnd()

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.batchEnd

docs.title: mintmousse.buildPage
docs.text: Used to add a preconfigured tab to your console page. Useful to quickly setting up a repeating-tab, or to use someone else's preset.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: tab = mintmousse.buildPage( requirePath, config, index )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: requirePath string The require path to the page code you want to add to the console. config table ({ }) The config table given to the page to set up the page. If it contains tabName field, it will use that for the name of the tab it will create, or one defined by the page itself if it's nil. index number (nil) The position in the navbar you want the new tab to appear at. Default behaviour is to add it to the end of the navbar.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: tab Tab The newly create and populated tab.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.buildPage("libs.MintMousse.pages.sandbox")

local logTab = mintmousse.buildPage("libs.MintMousse.pages.console", { tabName = "Logs", maxLines = 32 })
logTab:newCard({ size = 5 })
  :addCardFooter({ text = "This is the console page, but I can still add components to it" })

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Controller

docs.title: mintmousse.clearWhitelist
docs.text:
Clears all currently added to the whitelist, so you can start fresh.

Active Connections

Removing a rule does not disconnect clients who are already connected. The whitelist is only checked at the moment a new connection is attempted.

Lockout Risk

Calling this function while the server is running will prevent any new connections from being established, including those from localhost. Ensure you add a new rule immediately after clearing if you intend to keep the console accessible.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.clearWhitelist()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.clearWhitelist()

mintmousse.addWhitelist("localhost")

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.removeFromWhitelist
  • mintmousse.addToWhitelist

docs.title: mintmousse.getThreadID
docs.text: Get the unique ID for the current thread.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: threadID = mintmousse.getThreadID()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: threadID string The threadID assigned to the current thread.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local threadID = mintmousse.getThreadID()
print("Hello from " .. threadID)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Controller

docs.title: mintmousse.notify
docs.text: Send a notification to all connected clients to see timestamped. It will not appear for new clients.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.notify( message )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: message string or table The message to show, if you pass in a string, it will be set to the table's text argument.

docs.title: Message Table
docs.text: title string String used as the title of the notification. text string String used as the text of the notification.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.notify("A player went out of bounds!")

mintmousse.notify({
  title = "Player Ban",
  text = player:getName() .. " was banned by " .. admin:getName(),
})

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Controller

docs.title: mintmousse.removeFromWhitelist
docs.text:
Removes the given rules from the whitelist.

Active Connections

Removing a rule does not disconnect clients who are already connected. The whitelist is only checked at the moment a new connection is attempted.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.removeFromWhitelist( removals )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: removals string or table (nil) The rule(s) you want to remove. * If a string is passed, it is treated as a single rule. * If a table is passed, it must be a continuous array of strings.

docs.title: Rule Matching
docs.text:
To successfully remove a rule, the string must exactly match the format used when it was added (e.g. if you add a CIDR range, you must provide the same CIDR range to remove it).

Note, removing "localhost", "local", "127.0.0.1", or "::1" will remove both the IPv4 and IPv6 loopback entries simultaneously.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.removeFromWhitelist("192.167.4.27")

mintmousse.removeFromWhitelist({
  "localhost",
  "10.0.0.0/8",
})

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.addToWhitelist

docs.title: mintmousse.setIcon
docs.text:
Set the icon of the console (the little image shown in the window/tab) to make your project recognisable.

Don't have a ready-made image/logo? No problem - pass a simple table and the built in SVG generator will create a clean, emoji-based icon for you on the fly.

Browser behaviour

A known issue with the SVG Schema option is that SVG's on Chrome render a lot blurrier than that on Firefox.

I've noticed in Chromium it renders fine, not sure why Chrome wants to play up.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.setIcon( icon )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
icon string or table or Data

The icon source. The function handles four common use-cases in:

  1. SVG Schema: table

  2. A table describing a custom SVG icon. See definition below. This is designed to be the quick and easy, so it's options are limited.

  3. File Path: string

    • Path to an image file. Supported extensions: .png, .jpeg, .jpg, .svg, .ico

  4. Raw Image Data: string or Data

    • Binary image data for a PNG or JPEG file. Automatically detected by their standard magic-number headers. If you pass a Data, it is converted into a string first.

docs.title: SVG Schema Table
docs.text: shape string (nil) Background shape: "circle", "rectangle", "square", or omitted (uses a squircle - rounded square). Note "rectangle" and "square" are treated the same. insideColor or color string ("#95D7AB") Fill Colour of the icon. Accepts any standard web colour name ("red", "blue", etc.) or a hex code ("#RRGGBB" or "#RGB"). outsideColor string ("#4A7C59") Stroke (outline) colour. Same format as insideColor. Use "none" to disable the stroke entirely. strokeWidth number (3) Thickness of the stroke. Ignored if outsideColor is "none". emoji string ("🍮") Single emoji (or any single character) displayed in the centre. Note, multi-character emojis are supported, but no guarantee it will render correctly by browsers.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.setIcon("assets/console/icon.svg")
mintmousse.setIcon("assets/console/icon.ico")

mintmousse.setIcon(love.filesystem.read("assets/console/icon.png"))
mintmousse.setIcon(love.filesystem.read("assets/console/icon.jpeg"))

docs.title: SVG Schema
docs.text:
mintmousse.setIcon({ }) -- MintMousse's default, same as:
mintmousse.setIcon({
  shape        = nil, -- squircle
  insideColor  = "#95D7AB",
  outsideColor = "#4A7C59",
  strokeWidth  = 3,
  emoji        = "🍮",
})

mintmousse.setIcon({
  shape        = "circle",
  insideColor  = "#abf",
  outsideColor = "#f00",
  strokeWidth  = 2,
  emoji        = "🚀",
})

mintmousse.setIcon({
  color = "#cca486",
  emoji = "🍖",
  outsideColor = "none",
})

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.setIconRaw

docs.title: mintmousse.setIconRFG
docs.text:
This function provides integration with the Real Favicon Generator by accepting the ZIP file it produces. Instead of manually extracting dozens of icons files, and trying to individually pass them into MintMousse. The library mounts the archive and serves all required assets at the correct paths.

This approach supports a much broader range of platforms and devices than a the other basic single-icon setup, but in most cases isn't needed.

docs.title: Usage with Real Favicon Generator
docs.text:
1. Go to Real Favicon Generator and upload your master image (ideally a high-resolution PNG or SVG)
  2. Configure the options for the platforms you want to support (browser, iOS, Android, Windows, etc.)
  3. In the final step, set the Favicon path to match the MintMousse config FAVICON_PATH. The default is /favicon.
  4. Click Next, and for HTML, you should see a "Download your favicon package", which will give you the file to pass into this function.

MintMousse will automatically handle serving the contents of the ZIP so that all icons and supporting files (manifests, config XML, etc) are available at the expected URLs for the console.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.setIconRFG( filepath )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: filepath string Path to the .zip achieve generated by Real Favicon Generator. MintMousse will mount this archive.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: mintmousse.setIconRFG("assets/console/RFG.zip")

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Controller

docs.title: mintmousse.setIconRaw
docs.text:
Skip the validation of mintmousse.setIcon, and allows you to manually define the icon data and it's Content Type.

If you're feeling lost with this function, I do recommend sticking with mintmousse.setIcon to keep it simple.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.setIconRaw( icon, iconType )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: icon string The raw binary data of the image file. iconType string The MIMIE Type (Content Type) of the data. This is the "label" that tells the browser how to render the raw data you've given.

docs.title: Common Icon Content Types
docs.text:
If you aren't sure what to put here, find your extensions below:

File Extension Content Type (iconType) .png "image/png" .jpg/.jpeg "image/jpeg" .ico "image/x-icon" .svg "image/svg+xml" .gif "image/gif"

Why do I need this?

On the web, file extensions (like .png) don't actually matter; they're just flair to the file name. Without the Content Type label, the browser might treat your icon as plain text, and not display anything instead of the icon.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local file = love.filesystem.newFile("assets/console/icon.png", "r")
mintmousse.setIconRaw(file:read(), "image/png")
file:close()

mintmousse.setIconRaw(love.filesystem.read("assets/console.icon.svg"), "image/svg+xml")

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.setIcon

docs.title: mintmousse.setTitle
docs.text: Set the title of the web console, and the name in the tab. By default, the title is preset to "MintMousse".

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.setTitle( title )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: title string Sets the title of the console to the given string.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.setTitle("MintMousse")

mintmousse.setTitle("Game Console")

mintmousse.setTitle(love.window.getTitle() .. " Console")

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.start

docs.title: mintmousse.start
docs.text:
Used to start the web console's server so it can start serving clients.

Can only be called on the main thread.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.start( config )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: config table (nil) A table of config values to use on startup.

docs.title: Config Table
docs.text: title string The title to use for the web console. Also see mintmousse.setTitle. host string The host to bind the server to. If nil, default it is :: which tries to bind to IPv6 and IPv4, and fallbacks to * which tries to bind just to IPv4. See tcp:bind for documentation on host. port number The whole number port the server will be reachable on. If nil, default behaviour is to start at 8080 and will increment until it finds a port it can bind to. autoIncrement boolean If a port is given, if the server should try to increment the given port until it find ones. Default disabled. If port is nil, this will default to true. whitelist string or table A single rule, or an array of rules. See mintmousse.addToWhitelist for details.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.start({ whitelist = "localhost" }) -- Quickstart

mintmousse.start({ -- Default
  title = "MintMousse",
  host = nil,
  port = nil,
  autoIncrement = false,
  whitelist = nil, -- Note, with no whitelist, no connections will be accepted
})

mintmousse.start({
  title = love.window.getTitle() .. " Console",
  host = "::",
  port = 80,
  autoIncrement = false,
  whitelist = {
    "localhost",         -- Covers "local", "127.0.0.1", and "::1"
    "192.168.4.27",      -- Specific IPv4 address
    "192.168.0.0/16",    -- Private IPv4 Range (CIDR)
    "10.0.0.0/8",        -- Large Private IPv4 Range
    "2001:db8::/32",     -- IPv6 Range CIDR
    "2001:db8::cafe",    -- Single IPv6 Host
    "::ffff:127.16.0.1", -- IPv4-mapped IPv6 address support
  },
})

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.addToWhitelist

docs.title: mintmousse.stop
docs.text:
Stop and waits for the MintMousse thread to rejoin.

Can only be called on the main thread.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.stop( noWait )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
noWait boolean (false)

If we shouldn't wait for the thread to rejoin, and just send the quit command.

If you choose not to wait, you can wait later by calling mintmousse.wait

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.stop()

mintmousse.stop(true)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.wait

docs.title: mintmousse.wait
docs.text:
Waits for the thread to rejoin. Note, if mintmousse.stop hasn't been called, it will indefinitely block the main thread.

Can only be called on the main thread.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.wait()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.wait()

love.quit()
  mintmousse.stop(true)
  -- ...
  mintmousse.wait()
  return
end

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Controller
  • mintmousse.stop

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
MintMousse ships a powerful, thread-safe logging system that lets you:

  • Create named, color-aware loggers with easy hierarchy (module:submodule:minor)
  • Print to console with automatic ANSI stripping for files
  • Route logs anywhere (file, REST, custom sink, etc.)
  • Safely handle crashes via love.errorhandler so nothing is missed.

docs.title: Types
docs.text: Type Description Logger Logging instance

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Description mintmousse.newLogger Create a new named, colored logger object mintmousse.flushLogs Flush the log buffer (thread-safe) mintmousse.logUncaughtError Catch errors in love.errorhandler mintmousse.addLogSink Register a custom destination for all log messages on the current thread mintmousse.addGlobalLogSink Register a custom destination for all log messages across the program mintmousse.cleanupTraceback Removes unhelpful entries from the traceback

docs.title: Enums
docs.text: Enum Description Color Color definitions for the logging module

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
-- 1. Create & extend loggers (the most common use)
local moduleLogger = mintmousse.newLogger("module", "cyan")
moduleLogger:info("Module initialized")

local subLogger = moduleLogger:extend("submodule", "bright_blue")
subLogger:warning("You're getting the hang of it now", 5, { ["foo"] = "bar" })

local minorLogger = subLogger:extend("minor")
minorLogger:debug("I print the line I'm on - useful during development!")

print("Quick out") -- works like `logger:debug`, but super quick and dirty


-- 2. Catch all error that don't route the expected way
love.errorhandler = function(msg)
  msg = tostring(msg)
  mintmousse.logUncaughtError(msg) -- automatically logs and flushes
  -- ... error screen here
end


-- 3. Advanced: send logs to a server
mintmousse.addLogSink(function(level, logger, time, debugInfo, ...)
  if level == "error" or level == "fatal" then
    -- send to your analytic endpoint
  end
end)

The (Logger):extend method automatically builds the [module:submodule:minor] prefix to quickly organise your logs.

docs.title: mintmousse.addGlobalLogSink
docs.text:
Used to add a custom sink to direct log messages from across all threads in a single place. This function can only be used on the main thread.

I recommend checking out the logging sinks that the library implements to fully understand how you can implement your own.

Thread behaviour

This function can only be called on the main thread. If you can architect it, it's recommended to use the pre-thread sinks added via mintmousse.addLogSink. So, then your log isn't having to wait to be processed on the main thread.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.addGlobalLogSink( sink, flushFunc )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: sink function A function, with the signature (level, logger, time, debugInfo, message...). See below for details flushFunc function A function, with the signature (forced). See mintmousse.flushLogs for details

docs.title: Sink Function Signature
docs.text:
level level A string indicating the level of the log, "info", "warning", "error", "fatal", "debug". logger logger

The logger that created the log.

You shouldn't try to use any other function of the logger when it is in a sink other than (Logger):getAncestry and (Logger).inspect.

time number UNIX time in seconds, with microseconds. By default, this value is obtained by socket.gettime to get an accurate system time. debugInfo string or nil Depending on the level, and the config -- TODO link, a simple traceback string is passed along the lines of funcName@fileName#lineNumber, this format isn't guaranteed, for example code in the file scope will be fileName#lineNumber as they aren't contained within a named function. message... ANY The varargs of the message passed from the log function.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Not necessarily working code, but an idea. 

local http = require("socket.http")

local logs = { }
mintmousse.addGlobalLogSink(
  function(level, logger, time, debugInfo, ...) -- Sink function
    if (level == "error" or info == "fatal") then
      table.insert(logs, table.concat({ ... }, " "))
    end
  end,
  function(forced) -- Flush function
    http.request({
        url = "http://example.com/endpoint/?logs=" .. table.concat(logs, "&"),
        method = "POST",
      })
  end
)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • mintmousse.addLogSink

docs.title: mintmousse.addLogSink
docs.text:
Used to add a custom sink to direct log messages to where ever you want, for the current thread that the function is ran on.

I recommend checking out the logging sinks that the library implements to fully understand how you can implement your own.

Thread behaviour

This function is used to route logs of the thread that this function is ran on. If you just want to set a logging sink once, and handle all logs from all threads see mintmousse.addGlobalLogSink. This function is recommended over global sinks for performance.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.addLogSink( sink, flushFunc )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: sink function A function, with the signature (level, logger, time, debugInfo, message...). See below for details flushFunc function A function, with the signature (forced). See mintmousse.flushLogs for details

docs.title: Sink Function Signature
docs.text:
level level A string indicating the level of the log, "info", "warning", "error", "fatal", "debug". logger logger

The logger that created the log.

You shouldn't try to use any other function of the logger when it is in a sink other than (Logger):getAncestry and (Logger).inspect.

time number UNIX time in seconds, with microseconds. By default, this value is obtained by socket.gettime to get an accurate system time. debugInfo string or nil Depending on the level, and the config -- TODO link, a simple traceback string is passed along the lines of funcName@fileName#lineNumber, this format isn't guaranteed, for example code in the file scope will be fileName#lineNumber as they aren't contained within a named function. message... ANY The varargs of the message passed from the log function.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.addLogSink(
  function(level, logger, time, debugInfo, ...) -- Sink function
    if (level == "info" or info == "warning") and
        and math.floor(time) and not debugInfo then
      local num = select('#', ...)
      io.stdout:write(num .. "\n")
    end
  end,
  function(forced) -- Flush function
    io.stdout:flush()
  end
)

logger:info("Hello", "World", 0, nil, { }) -- 6\n
logger:warning("Foo", "Bar") -- 2\n

mintmousse.flushLogs()

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • mintmousse.addGlobalLogSink

docs.title: mintmousse.cleanupTraceback
docs.text:
Used to remove unhelpful traces in the traceback. It removes internal Love and MintMousse traces reducing the noise you could see.

Removes internal Love and MintMousse traces from a traceback, leaving only the parts that matter.

This makes error messages much cleaner and easier to read, especially when something goes wrong deep inside your project.

Before: 

stack traceback:
        [love "boot.lua"]:479: in function <[love "boot.lua"]:475>
        [C]: in function 'error'
        MintMousse/logging/logger.lua:98: in function 'error'
        main.lua:5: in main chunk
        [C]: in function 'require'
        [love "boot.lua"]:444: in function <[love "boot.lua"]:173>
        [C]: in function 'xpcall'
        [love "boot.lua"]:492: in function <[love "boot.lua"]:483>
        [C]: in function 'xpcall'
        [love "boot.lua"]:515: in function <[love "boot.lua"]:470>
After: 
Traceback:
    [C]: in function 'error'
    main.lua:5: in main chunk

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: cleanedTrace = mintmousse.cleanupTraceback( trace )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: trace string A traceback string, usually obtained from debug.traceback()

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: cleanedTrace string The same traceback with all internal Love and MintMousse traces removed.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
See MintMousse's implemented errorhandler to see it in a love.errorhandler situation. 

local trace = debug.traceback()
local cleanedTrace = mintmousse.cleanupTraceback(trace)
print(cleanedTrace)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • mintmousse.logUncaughtError

docs.title: Color
docs.text:
The color field within the logging module expects either a string, or a table with two fields fg (foreground, e.g. text) and bg (background, e.g. a 'highlight'). If just a string is passed, it is used like the fg argument, and bg will default to the console's background.

ANSI color support in Windows Console only became reliable with Windows 10 build 10586 (late 2015) and later; older versions (or very old Windows 7/8) usually show plain white/gray output.

docs.title: Constants
docs.text:
The following are string fields that are allowed. Their exact HEX color depends on the rendering console, but here are approximate PuTTY colors:

  • black
  • red
  • green
  • yellow
  • blue
  • magenta
  • cyan
  • white
  • bright_black
  • bright_red
  • bright_green
  • bright_yellow
  • bright_blue
  • bright_magenta
  • bright_cyan
  • bright_white
  • reset

reset

reset is a special 'color', which reverts to what the default is of your console, for example usually white text for foreground, or black background.

Quick tip: white vs bright_white

In many terminals, white renders as silvery gray (~#bbbbbb), while bright_white is pure white (#ffffff).

For regular log text, you should use white as it's more comfortable on the eyes - especially on OLED/AMOLED screens or when reading for long periods. Reserve bright_white for emphasis, errors, or when you need maximum contrast (e.g. white text on a red background).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local logger = mintmousse.newLogger("foo", "cyan")

local loggerBar = logger:extend("bar", "green")

local loggerFatal = mintmousse.newLogger("FATAL", { fg = "bright_white", bg = "red" })

local loggerInvert = loggerFatal:extend("Inverted", {
  fg = "black",
  bg = "bright_white",
})

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • mintmousse.newLogger
  • (Logger):extend

docs.title: mintmousse.flushLogs
docs.text: Thread-safe function to call flush function callbacks per sink. On the main thread, it also processes global sinks.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.flushLogs( forced )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: forced boolean (false) Used to override the thread lock and immediately flush the buffer. This parameter is used internally during a crash state, and not recommended to be used in practice.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
mintmousse.flushLogs()

mintmousse.flushLogs(true)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Logging

docs.title: Level
docs.text: The log levels that can occur, and where they can originate from.

docs.title: Constants
docs.text: Level Origins info (Logger):info warning (Logger):warning debug (Logger):debug & print (global function) error (Logger):error fatal mintmousse.logUncaughtError

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • mintmousse.addLogSink

docs.title: mintmousse.logUncaughtError
docs.text:
Use within love.errorhandler, to make sure all thrown errors are caught and directed into the sinks. See the errorhandler that MintMousse implements, but TLDR; it's put in place of error_printer in the default love.errorhandler.

Messages caught using this function will appear as the log level fatal indicated the halt to the program.

Error Handler

MintMousse by default sets it's own error handler which already implements it. If you override love.errorhandler yourself later with your own implementation, you will need to add this function yourself. Otherwise, the logs may appear incomplete at time of crash, or be out of order.

To disable MintMousse setting it's own error handler, see the config. -- TODO link

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.logUncaughtError( message, tracebackLayer )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: message string The reported error message tracebackLayer number (0) Increase to reduce the generated traceback to correctly report the cause of the crash. 'layer' 0 is when it is directly called within love.errorhandler.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
love.errorhandler = function(msg)
  msg = tostring(msg)

  mintmousse.logUncaughtError(msg)
  -- or
  mintmousse.logUncaughtError(msg, 0)
  -- or
  (function()
    mintmousse.logUncaughtError(msg, 1)
  end)()

  -- etc..
end

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Logging

docs.title: mintmousse.newLogger
docs.text: Creates a new logger object.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: mintmousse.newLogger( name, color )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: name string Identifier shown in the logs color color ("white") Color used to highlight the name in consoles that support color highlighting

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: logger logger A new object of a logger

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local logger = mintmousse.newLogger("Level")

local logger = mintmousse.newLogger("Joystick", "magenta")

local logger = mintmousse.newLogger("Physics", { fg = "white", bg = "blue" })

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • (Logger):extend

docs.title: Logger
docs.text: The object to be able to log messages out.

docs.title: Constructors
docs.text: Constructors Description mintmousse.newLogger Create a new named, colored logger object (Logger):extend Create a new named, colored logger object with the parent object appearing before it

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Description (Logger):info Log an info level message (Logger):warning Log a warning level message (Logger):debug Log a debug level message (Logger):error Log an error level message (Logger):assert Test a condition, if false, log an error level message (Logger):getAncestry Used to get hierarchy of logger objects (Logger).inspect Used to look into a table

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Logging

docs.title: (Logger):assert
docs.text: Test a condition, if false, log an error level message.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: Logger:assert( condition, message... )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: condition boolean If condition is false, the message is promoted to an error message... ANY The varargs of the message passed to all the sinks. If you pass in a table, it will only print to a maximum depth of 3 tables.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Logger:assert("Hello" == "Hello", "World")

Logger:assert(false, "Hello World")

Logger:assert(not tbl, "Hello", 5, "World")

Logger:assert(isType(foo, "table"), nil, 1, "", true, false)

Logger:assert(not not tbl, { key = "value", ["foo"] = "bar" }) -- by default, max depth is 3, see Logger.inspect for larger depths

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • Logger
  • (Logger):error
  • (Logger).inspect

docs.title: (Logger):debug
docs.text:
Log a debug level message. This log level usually includes prints the line it is called on.

This is usually in the format of funcName@fileName#LineNumber, but not always. For example, if it was called it filescope, or couldn't find a name for the function it will just be fileName#LineNumber.

If you use the global function print, it will act like a debug log message. This behaviour can be disabled by checking the config. -- TODO link

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: Logger:debug( message... )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: message... ANY The varargs of the message passed to all the sinks. If you pass in a table, it will only print to a maximum depth of 3 tables.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Logger:debug("Hello World")

Logger:debug("Hello", "World")

Logger:debug("Hello", 5, "World")

Logger:debug(nil, 1, "", true, false)

Logger:debug({ key = "value", ["foo"] = "bar" }) -- by default, max depth is 3, see Logger.inspect for larger depths

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • Logger
  • (Logger).inspect

docs.title: (Logger):error
docs.text: Log an error level message. The default behaviour, unless the config is changed --TODO link, this will act like the typical global error function, and cause the program to halt and run the love.errorhandler.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: Logger:error( message... )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: message... ANY The varargs of the message passed to all the sinks. If you pass in a table, it will only print to a maximum depth of 3 tables.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Logger:error("Hello World")

Logger:error("Hello", "World")

Logger:error("Hello", 5, "World")

Logger:error(nil, 1, "", true, false)

Logger:error({ key = "value", ["foo"] = "bar" }) -- by default, max depth is 3, see Logger.inspect for larger depths

docs.title: (Logger):extend
docs.text:
Builds upon an existing logger object to make a 'chained' name to appear within the logs.

For example, the default behaviour is to 'chain' left-to-right, [a:b:c] with each name appearing in the chosen color.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: logger = Logger:extend( name, color )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: name string Identifier shown in the logs. color color ("white") Color used to highlight the name in consoles that support color highlighting.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: logger logger A new object of a logger.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local a = Logger:extend("a")

local b = a:extend("b", "blue")

local c = c:extend("c", { fg = "white", bg = "blue" })

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • Logger
  • mintmousse.newLogger

docs.title: (Logger):getAncestry
docs.text:
Used internally to get all hierarchy of the current Logger. The array is in "reverse", with the first logger at the first position, and the next one is the one the current Logger was (Logger):extended from.

This is useful if you're writing your own log sink.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: chain = Logger:getAncestry()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
ancestryData table

An array of the logger's ancestry information. ancestryData[1] will be the upmost root, ancestryData[#ancestryData] will be the Logger that called the function.

Each entry in the table, is a table with the two fields name string and colorDef color

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local ancestryData = Logger:getAncestry()
local prefixParts = { }
for _, node in ipairs(ancestryData) do
  table.insert(prefixParts, node.name)
end
print(table.concat(prefixParts, ":"))

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • Logger
  • (Logger):extend

docs.title: (Logger):info
docs.text: Log an info level message.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: Logger:info( message... )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: message... ANY The varargs of the message passed to all the sinks. If you pass in a table, it will only print to a maximum depth of 3 tables.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Nothing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Logger:info("Hello World")

Logger:info("Hello", "World")

Logger:info("Hello", 5, "World")

Logger:info(nil, 1, "", true, false)

Logger:info({ key = "value", ["foo"] = "bar" }) -- by default, max depth is 3, see Logger.inspect for larger depths

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • Logger
  • (Logger).inspect

docs.title: (Logger).inspect
docs.text:
Used to look into a given table, it will prevent circular dependency, and use a max depth to prevent "too much".

Warning

No two returns may be equal, due to internally using the global pairs function which is known to not always return the same order.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: str = Logger.inspector( value, level )

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: value ANY If a non-table type is given, it will return it using tostring. level depth or number ("light") The maximum recursive depth to look into a table if it contains tables. "light" goes a depth of 1, "deep" goes a depth of 3, and number lets you fine tune how much depth.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: str string A formatted string of the given table, or non-table value, to the specified depth.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
local tbl = {
  foo = "bar",
  tbl = { "foo", "bar" }
}

local str = Logger.inspect(tbl, "light")
print(str)

Logger:info(Logger.inspect(tbl, "deep"))

Logger:info(Logger.inspect(tbl, 3))

local a = { }
local b = { }
a["b"] = b
b["a"] = a

Logger:warning(Logger.inspect(a, "deep"))

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Logging
  • Logger
